coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots based long-persistent chemiluminescence system for ascorbic acid imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hongjun
Wang, Qin
Shen, Qinpeng
Liu, Xin
Li, Wang
Nie, Zhou
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: High photo-intensity and sluggish flight attenuation are important to highly sensitive chemluminescence imaging. Herein, we present a copper ion catalyzed long-persistent chemiluminescent imaging system of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) for ascorbic acid detection in fruit. NGQDs as luminescent probe are fabricated, emitting out chemluminescence with the direct oxidation by H2O2. In addition, Cu2+ ion enlarges over two order magnitudes of NGQDs CL intensity (214 times) due to its catalyzed Fenton-like reaction for H2O2 decomposition, and displaying unique specificity against other metal ions. As a result, the twinkling luminescence of NGQDs is boosted and changes to hold persistent with small decay in the presence of copper ion exhibiting potential for CL imaging. As an imaging model, a visual sensor based on Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 is developed for AA quantitative monitoring with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.5μM (S/N=3) and applied in real AA detection in fruit. The CL imaging method demonstrated with high stability and proper sensitivity would provide a convenient and visual tool for AA determination, displaying promising candidates for imaging sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Chemiluminescence
Ascorbic acid imaging
Copper ion

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pH and Cu2+ concentration on CL activity
Cu2+ ion specific enhancing CL of NGQDs/H2O2 system
CL imaging detection of ascorbic acid in fruit

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21305037, 21475037, and 21235002), the Excellent Youth Foundation of Hunan Scientific Committee (No. 2015JJ1005), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Typical TEM image and (B) high-resolution TEM image of the prepared NGQDs. (C) Wide scan XPS spectrum of N-GQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of the N1s (D) and C1s (E) in NGQDs, respectively, and their related curve-fitted components.

(A) The CL kinetic curve of NGQDs/H2O2 system (red line) and the corresponding control condition (4μgmL−1 NGQDs, 20mMH2O2). (B) The CL kinetic curve of Cu2+ enhanced NGQDs/H2O2 system and the enlarged inserted CL kinetic curve of NGQDs/H2O2 system (500μM Cu2+, 2μgmL−1 NGQDs, 5mMH2O2). (C) Chemiluminescence spectra of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system and the corresponding control condition. Insertion is the normalized chemiluminescence (red line) and fluorescence (black line) of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system. CL kinetic curve added 200μM of ascorbic acid (D), 100μM of thiourea (E) and SOD (F) respectively into the Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The CL kinetic curve of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system and the enlarged inserted CL kinetic curve of NGQDs/H2O2 system under different pH (from 6.0 to 9.0) condition. (B) The effect various metal ion and metal ion complexes (500μM) on CL of NGQDs/H2O2 system.

(A) CL images of NGQDs in the presence of 500μM Cu2+ with different concentration of H2O2. (B) CL images of NGQDs in the presence of 5mMH2O2 with different concentration of Cu2+. (C) CL image at various concentrations of ascorbic acid (the doted circles is the selected area for CL intensity value evaluating using the animal imaging software) and (D) the relevant luminescence intensity ratio plot at various concentrations of ascorbic acid (I0 and I represents to the signal intensity value of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system in the absence and presence of AA addition).

(A) The quenching effect of 1mM glucose, oxalic acid (OA), tartaric acid (TA), malic acid (MA), citric acid (CA) and 100μM AA on the CL intensity of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system. (B) CL images of after different amount of apple, apricot and pear sample addition of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system.

The detection results of ascorbic acid tabulated from 2μL addition of real sample as showed in Fig. 5B (a and b represents the results obtained from standard method and the proposed method, respectively).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots based long-persistent chemiluminescence system for ascorbic acid imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Tobacco Chemistry of Yunnan Province, China Tobacco Yunnan Industrial Co., Ltd, Kunming 650231, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Tobacco Chemistry of Yunnan Province, China Tobacco Yunnan Industrial Co., Ltd

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High photo-intensity and sluggish flight attenuation are important to highly sensitive chemluminescence imaging. Herein, we present a copper ion catalyzed long-persistent chemiluminescent imaging system of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) for ascorbic acid detection in fruit. NGQDs as luminescent probe are fabricated, emitting out chemluminescence with the direct oxidation by H2O2. In addition, Cu2+ ion enlarges over two order magnitudes of NGQDs CL intensity (214 times) due to its catalyzed Fenton-like reaction for H2O2 decomposition, and displaying unique specificity against other metal ions. As a result, the twinkling luminescence of NGQDs is boosted and changes to hold persistent with small decay in the presence of copper ion exhibiting potential for CL imaging. As an imaging model, a visual sensor based on Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 is developed for AA quantitative monitoring with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.5μM (S/N=3) and applied in real AA detection in fruit. The CL imaging method demonstrated with high stability and proper sensitivity would provide a convenient and visual tool for AA determination, displaying promising candidates for imaging sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, attentions are focused on highly sensitive and high-resolution optical imaging techniques construction with the purpose of visible tracing or quantitative analysis for different targets (Benic et al., 2015; Stender et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2013). During the past decades, photoluminescence imaging recognition the signal from fluorescent materials (organic fluorophores or luminescent semiconductors) becomes the commonly adopted imaging approach (Li et al., 2013). Despite they are usefulness, urgent requirement for an external light source and the excessive auto-fluorescence derived from an external illumination is still confronted which constrains their wide applications to some extent. Chemiluminescence (CL) arises from annihilation of chemically produced electronic excited state, having been a versatile and sensitive potent tool in diverse analytical fields (Lin et al., 2013). Comparing with photoluminescence, CL exhibits significant advantages in terms of needless an exterior light source, which able to minimize background interferences caused by an exterior light excitation and simplify the instruments, endowing it beneficial to imaging and sensing (Li et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2015b).

For CL imaging, high photo-intensity and sluggish flight attenuation are important to highly sensitive and real time imaging. Traditional CL imaging reactions are limited to a certain molecular systems, such as luminol and their derivates, peroxy-oxalate derivatives, acridinium ester, tris(2,20-bipyridine) ruthenium(II), bis(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl) oxalate (TCPO) and other designed organic dye (Chen et al., 2015; Francis et al., 2004; Griesbeck et al., 2015; Li et al., 2016; Lin et al., 2013). Most chemiluminescent reactions generate weak luminescence as low quantum efficiencies, and decline quickly after dozens of seconds needing cautious signal collection process by means of increasing exposure time of CCD detector. Hence, catalysis such as metal ions, nano-materials and enzymes are resorted to strengthen the persistence and brightness for imaging application (Hosseini et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2015; Ouyang et al., 2016; Zheng et al., 2016). For example, horseradish peroxidase (HRP) enzymatic enhances the chemiluminescent reaction between hydrogen peroxide and luminol becoming a definite project for CL intensity amplification particularly in bio-sensing CL imaging. However, this strategy requirement for an enzyme is limited to the enzymatic activity, partially constrains their imaging applications. For most organic chemiluminescent systems, the self-consumption of organic luminophor within CL procedures inevitably results in emissive intensity decay, in return brings about signal fluctuation during CL imaging process especially at low concentration. Recently, inorganic luminescent nano-materials capture considerable attentions on account of their superior photo-stability and high quantum ratio (Lin et al., 2011; Poznyak et al., 2004). Alternatively, nano-material luminophor retains in almost minimum loss in numbers able to overcome self damage induced emissive fluctuation because emissions derived from annihilation between electron and hole injected free radical, which would improve the sensitivity and stability of luminescent system. Therefore, chemiluminescent system on basis of carbon materials with the aid of some oxidant has been prepared because of their superiority in chemical inertness, low cost and resistance to photo-bleaching capability (Dong et al., 2014; Dou et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2011, 2012; Tang et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2013). Those studies give insight investigations into the CL mechanism, while little about their CL imaging application.

As the former consideration, a type of CL imaging system owning adequate intensity, light brightness and photo-stability is designed employing carbon materials as luminophor and inorganic catalysis to boost CL reactions rate. In this context, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were fabricated and demonstrated to generate CL from the direct oxidation by H2O2. In addition, we here revealed the specific and potent catalytic enhancement activity of Cu2+ ion towards the NGQDs/H2O2 CL system. Surprisingly, Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system shows sluggish emissive decline, exhibiting promising potential for convenient CL imaging sensing. The mechanisms of Cu2+ ion enhanced chemiluminescence of NGQDs/H2O2 system are discussed in detail as well. Afterward, Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system was studied as an imaging model for sensing construction. Based on the radical clear ability of ascorbic acid (AA), an imaging sensor is developed for AA quantitative evaluation and applied to AA detection in fruit. This study not only provides a category of optical probes for CL imaging but also reveals the underlying principle to enhance the brightness of chemiluminescent systems. NGQDs considering as a future generation of green luminophor would be a promising candidate for imaging and bio-sensing.

Graphite powder (99.9%, 325 meshes) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. H2O2 and thiourea were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methylene blue (MB), 2,2′-Azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) (ABTS) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Superoxide dismutase (SOD) and HRP were gained from Sigma-Aldrich. Commercial AA kit was purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute (Nanjing, China). Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) were prepared using 0.2M KH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. The metal ions are used with their chloride. All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q system (18.2MΩcm) was used in all runs.

The batch experiments were performed on an MPI-A multifunctional electrochemical and chemiluminescent analytical system (Remex, Xi’an, China) at room temperature. The detection and analysis of signals were performed using an animal imaging instrument (IVIS Lumina XR, Caliper Co., U.S.A.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with Al Kα (1486.6eV) as the X-ray source. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F30 ST instruments (FEI Co., U.S.A.) operating at 300kV. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a 1cm path length quartz cuvette on the QuantaMasterTM4 fluorescence spectrometer (PTI, London, Canada). Kinetic curves for Fenton reaction were recorded in Synergy HTX multi-mode reader.

Nitrogen element was doped into the carbon framework of graphene oxide by ammonium treatment with the aid of H2O2. Briefly, 10mL graphene oxide (GO) was initially incubated with ammonium hydroxide and H2O2 to doping nitrogen element into nano-sized graphene oxide (1mgmL−1) according to the reported method (Jiang et al., 2013; Tetsuka et al., 2012). Then, 20mL of the as prepared nano-sized graphene oxide was reduced using hydrazine in 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave with hydrothermal method. After cooling to room temperature, the desired NGQDs sheets suspension were collected after filtered out with 0.22µm millipore membrane. Residual hydrazine was boiled away using rotary evaporation distillation upon the NGQDs sheets ethanol suspension and then the brown NGQDs sheets were freeze-dried. Finally, the frozen substance was configured to the solution with water and stored at 4°C for following use.

The AA content in fruit samples were extracted using the following procedure. Briefly, two-grams of peeled fruit samples were randomly taken and pounded in the centrifugal tube. Then 1mL of ultrapure water was added into the tube, mechanically shaked for 1h and centrifugated at 8000rpm for 15min. The supernatant was collected for immediate CL imaging assays. For commercial AA kit detection, the obtaining concentrated supernatant was filtered with 0.45µm millipore membrane and diluted 10 times for further detection according to the given guidelines.

For Vc pills pretreatment, one pellet of Vc pill (the given AA content is 100mg) was grounded into powder, dissolved into 10mL distilled water, mechanically shaken for 30min and centrifugated at 8000rpm for 15min. Then, the obtained suspension was collected and diluted for 1000 times for immediate CL imaging assays. Finally, 1μL of extracting solution was used for AA imaging detection. For Vc drinks (the given AA content is 100mg per 445 mililiter) imaging assays, briefly, 40μL of Vc drink solution which filtered with 0.45µm membrane was used for AA imaging assays.

CL measurements were conducted on an MPI-A multifunctional electrochemical and chemiluminescent analytical system at room temperature and the PMT was adjusted to −800V. For a typical CL measurement, 2μL of NGQDs solutions and a certain amount of 0.2M PBS was firstly injected into each well of microplate and mixed each other. Then H2O2 and Cu2+ solutions were injected into the solution consisting of the final CL detective solution. The absolute detection solution was fixed to 200μL with 0.2M PBS (pH 8.0) containing 2μgmL−1 NGQDs, 5mMH2O2 and 500μM Cu2+ solutions except for special version. CL spectrum was measured on the QuantaMasterTM4 fluorescence spectrometer with the excitation source shut off. For CL imaging experiments, detection solution was injected into 96-wellplates. The post-processing of the original images were executed on an animal imager software by the self-taken circular data reading tool, which able to provide values of the circular selected area.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image ( Fig. 1A) shows the resulting NGQDs are relatively uniform in size and exhibit a relatively narrow size distribution between 2 and 5nm with an average diameter of 3nm (inset of Fig. 1A), providing the first confirmation of formed nanosize pieces during the synthesis of NGQDs. Afterward, high-resolution TEM image reveals excellent crystallinity with a lattice parameter of 0.21nm (Fig. 1B) of NGQDs, which agrees with the in-plane lattice fringes (100) of graphene (Cai et al., 2015).

XPS is a proficient and frequently used method to investigate the features of elementary species in carbon materials, and is performed to determine the composition of the as-produced NGQDs. The wide scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 1C) demonstrated that the NGQDs contain carbon (C1s peak at ~284.8eV), oxygen (O1s peak at ~531.8eV), and nitrogen (N1s peak at ~400.9eV) elements. The appearance of N1s peak confirms the successful incorporation of N atoms into graphene (Li et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2015a; Zhu et al., 2016). The deconvolution of the N1s peak (Fig. 1D) gives more elaborate information about nitrogen functional groups upon NGQDs. The high-resolution N1s spectrum indicates the presence of pyridinic N (~399.9eV) and graphitic N (~401.5eV) atoms which is ~54% and 46%, respectively (Cai et al., 2015). To further evaluate the nitrogen configuration in NGQDs, the C1s XPS has been deconvoluted into five peaks at 284.3, 284.8, 285.4, 286.3 and 288.5eV (Fig. 1E), attributing to C˭C, C−C, C−N, C−O, and C˭O bonds, respectively (Casanovas et al., 1996; Hu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2015a). These results suggested the presence of oxygen and nitrogen-contained groups in the NGQDs.

The prepared NGQDs generated obvious CL signal and declined rapidly when mixed with H2O2 (20mM) under pH 8.0 PBS buffer as recorded in Fig. 2A. In addition, decreasing the dosage to half of NGQDs and conducted under a moderate concentration of H2O2 (5mM), a rapid and faint CL signal still achieved (inset of Fig. 2B), further verifying its chemiluminescent property. Actually, the quick decline in CL intensity makes NGQDs/H2O2 system not benefit to CL imaging. Attempting to acquire stable and strong enough CL signal with legible brightness for imaging application, Cu2+ was selected and added into the NGQDs/H2O2 system. Surprisingly, the interaction of Cu2+ and H2O2 in the presence of NGQDs is accompanied by strong and stable CL signal (Fig. 2B). In light of the reaction between NGQDs and H2O2 only brought in weak and transient CL signal (inset of Fig. 2B), indicative of the contribution of H2O2 to the high CL is inappreciable. In order to elucidate the role of Cu2+ toward NGQDs/H2O2 coexistence system, HRP was added into NGQDs/H2O2 coexistence system to provide abundant free radical by catalytic decomposition of H2O2, and consequently noticeable enhancement in CL intensity observes as well (Fig. S1). Therefore, Cu2+ may act as catalytic role analogous to HRP. Meanwhile, the related reactive radical species that derived from Cu2+ catalyzed Fenton-like reaction toward H2O2 decomposition might be the major pathway for boosting the CL intensity. It is worth noting that the CL behavior of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system changes to relatively stable with about 3.5% decline in total intensity after 120s continuous scanning as showed in Fig. 2B. In particular, CL intensity of Cu2+ catalyzed NGQDs/H2O2 system is more stable than HRP catalyzed NGQDs/H2O2 system with over 69% signal declines (Fig. S1). The advantages of both relative steady signal strength and sluggish emissive decline in CL intensity will minimize signal deflections, are benefit to accurate imaging collection.

To further investigate the unique CL phenomenon, CL spectrum was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with xenon-lamp off. It should be noted that only one maximum emission peak centered around 450nm was obtained (Fig. 2C), which is uniform with the fluorescent spectra of NGQDs (inset of Fig. 2C) rather than the emission band of dimol singlet-oxygen (1O2)2*(381, 477 and 578nm) (Adam et al., 2005). In addition, control experimental results showed that the coexistent substrates containing Cu2+ possess none of CL emission, indicating the virtual luminophor role of NGQDs. Afterward, NaN3 as a typical (1O2)2* scavenger, exhibits little quenching effect on the intensity of the system (Fig. S2) ruled out the interference derived from (1O2)2*, further verifying NGQDs are really the emissive unit (Tang et al., 2013). Therefore, we conclude that the light emission of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system originated from NGQDs and strengthen by Cu2+ ions. The performances of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system for several kinds of radical scavengers were investigated aiming to discover the involved radical species among CL process. Firstly, ascorbic acid (AA) as a common scavenger for free radicals quenches the CL intensity observably, indicating the radical reaction pathway of the Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system (Fig. 2D). Secondly, the CL intensity were effectively quenched upon addition of thiourea (Fig. 2E) and SOD (Fig. 2F) which were widely accepted as a hydroxyl radical (•OH) and a •O2 − anionic radicals scavenger (Zheng et al., 2016,) respectively, suggesting the key role of the hydroxyl radical and •O2 −.

Under alkaline condition, H2O2 is unstable and tend to decompose into some reactive radial species (Kremer, 2003). Traditionally, HO2 − is firstly produced from H2O2 deprotonation (reaction 1, see below), initiates the reactive oxygen chain reaction as HO2 − is unstable in aqueous solution, and leads to the generation of reactive oxygen radical •O2 −, •OH and HO2 • radicals (reaction 2 and 3) (Zhang et al., 2012). It is reported that oxidant radicals, such as •OH and HO2 •, can inject holes into the valence band of nano-material such as CdTe and carbon dots (Lin et al., 2011; Poznyak et al., 2004; Wang et al., 2005). Hence, hole injection from •OH into NGQDs is capable, accordingly formation of positive radical NGQDs(h
+ ) (reaction 4). As •O2 − radicals can be served as electron donors and easily inject an electron into the conduction band of some quantum dot (Poznyak et al., 2004; Wang et al., 2005). It is reasonable to deduce that •O2 − injects electron into NGQDs producing negatively charged radical NGQDs(e
− ) (reaction 5). The charged NGQDs radials (from electron injection and hole injection) collide upon electron annihilation give rise to NGQDs in the excited state NGQDs*(reaction 6), then the electron from the excited state transfer back to the ground state accompanied with illumination (reaction 7). Hence, it can be speculated that the decomposition of H2O2 drives the CL of NGQDs. Under alkalescent condition (pH 8.0), H2O2 decomposes at a sluggish speed accompanied by insufficient reactive oxygen radical to drive excited state NGQDs* forming. Hence, NGQDs emit dim CL intensity and decline quickly in the presence of H2O2. Depending on the CL mechanism of NGQDs/H2O2 system, CL intensity is significantly dependant on the generation rates of NGQDs*. So formation of reactive oxygen radical is essential to CL intensity of NGQDs/H2O2 system. Earlier studies showed that Cu2+ could catalyze H2O2 decomposition to reactive complex, which increased the production rate of •OH and •O2 − following a copper catalyzed Fenton-like reaction (reactions 8–10) (Perez-Benito, 2004; Pham et al., 2013), thereby enlarged the excited state production and boosted the CL of NGQDs. Based on the above discussion, the possible CL mechanism of copper ion induced Fenton-like reaction to NGQDs CL system is summarized as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and Cu2+ concentration on CL activity
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further explore the CL performance of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system and excavate its possible application potential, the pH effects on the CL intensity of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system were studied. NGQDs were firstly added into the solution containing 5mMH2O2 with different pH values PBS buffer (pH 6.0−9.0), and then Cu2+ was added into NGQDs/H2O2 coexistence solution to obtain the real-time enhancing CL intensities. As showed in Fig. 3A, CL intensity both of NGQDs/H2O2 and Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system increased with increasing the pH, which is in agreement with earlier studies because the production rate of intermediate radicals are accelerated and stable at high pH (Kremer, 2003). For the Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system, HO2 − productive rate is accelerated because H2O2 deprotonation reaction (reaction 1) converts to easier as pH condition transfer to more caustic, which results in enhanced radical production for the following consecutive reactions (reactions 4–10). It is worth noting that CL intensity of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system exhibited relatively stable and persistent in the pH ranges of 7.0–9.0, indicating its superior application capacity for CL imaging among wide pH value especially near to physiological conditions. Moreover, the CL emission of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system at pH 8.0 is 2 times larger than that of at pH 7.4, endowing easily signal collection for imaging equipment. Besides, the effects of Cu2+ concentration on the NGQDs/H2O2 CL system were also investigated and the results were showed in Fig. S3. The CL intensity increases with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, and becomes unstable with quick intensity decline in real-time signal when the concentration reaches up to 1.0mM. The quick decline would be due to the quenching ability of excess Cu2+. Considering suitable and stable CL intensities and moderate pH is beneficial to imaging and sensing, 500μM Cu2+ additions and pH 8.0 of was employed as the implementing optimum condition in following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ ion specific enhancing CL of NGQDs/H2O2 system
ce:section.ce:para:
H2O2 decomposition can be accelerated by low-valent transition-metal complex induced reaction for hydroxyl radical producing according to the following Fenton-like mechanism, i.e.
M
n +
+
H
2
O
2
→
M
( n + 1 ) +
+ O
H
−
+
O
•
H
, which contains series of consecutive radical reaction among the total reaction (Cmpski et al., 1971). Hence, effects of other metal ions and some metal ion complexes on the NGQDs/H2O2 system were investigated (Fig. 3B and S4). Cu2+ induces over two order magnitudes enhancement (214 times) towards the intensity of NGQDs/H2O2 CL system. Fe2+, Co2+ and Fe(CN)6 3- show slight enhance capability which is in line with their catalysis ability to Fenton or Fenton-like reaction (Graf et al., 1984; Kremer, 2003; Leonard et al., 1998). It is significant to explain Cu2+ induced specific selective amplification performance against other ions, especially Fe2+. Actually, under strong acid condition, Fe2+ exhibits a relative higher catalysis toward H2O2 decomposition for radical production compared with that of Cu2+(Fig. S5A). In addition, phosphate among the buffer evident suppresses the Fe2+ catalyzed Fenton reactions (Fig. S5B), and is consistent with the inhibition by phosphate ligand (Iwahashi et al., 1999). Afterward, Fe2+ catalyzed Fenton reactions is pH dependence, possess the highest rate at pH 4 (Fig. S6) attributing to formation of a more reactive Fe(OH)2 species (Neyens and Baeyens, 2003) and reduce to low catalytic performance at neutral solution (Fig. S6). In contrast, phosphate exhibits none inhibition toward Cu2+ catalyzed the Fenton-like reactions (Fig. S7A), and the catalytic activity of Cu2+ increases with pH increasing (Fig. S7B). Moreover, under the CL assaying condition (0.2M PBS, pH 8.0), Cu2+ exhibits faster radical production rate than the condition in presence of equal Fe2+ addition (Fig. S8). The production rates of radical species derived from Cu2+ catalyzed Fenton-like reactions is higher than that of Co2+ because the catalytic activity toward Fenton-like reactions induced by Cu2+ is superior to Co2+ under basic condition (Strlič et al., 2003). Consequently, in comparison to Fe2+ and Co2+, Cu2+ induces the largest CL enhancement for NGQDs/H2O2 CL system. Other metal ions almost have a little effect on the CL NGQDs/H2O2 system. In words, the Cu2+ exhibit excellent specificity amplification for the CL NGQDs/H2O2 system which will be beneficial for CL imaging application.

In order to investigate the imaging ability of the special Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system, the amount of H2O2 and Cu2+ crucial to NGQDs CL intensity was optimized. As showed in Fig. 4A and B, insufficient CL signal was noted at absence either H2O2 or Cu2+, indicating their synergistic effects on NGQDs CL imaging. With regard to the impact of H2O2 concentration (Fig. 4A), the image brightness is deepen with increasing of H2O2 concentration, and is saturated when the concentration increase reaching to 5mM. When Cu2+ were added into NGQDs/H2O2 system (Fig. 4B), the image brightness is increase as Cu2+ increasing to 1mM owning to the catalytic ability toward H2O2 decomposition by Fenton-like reaction, and decrease as the concentration approach to 2mM as the quenching effect derived from large amount of Cu2+(Xu et al., 2010). Therefore, 1mM Cu2+ and 5mMH2O2 were chosen as the optimal reaction concentration for CL imaging sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL imaging detection of ascorbic acid in fruit
ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA) is an important micronutrient in human diet, and functions as a key coenzyme in many metabolic pathways and a potent antioxidant against oxidative damages which have been implicated in many chronic diseases (Bendich et al., 1986; Carr and Frei, 1999). The supplement of AA is proved to be favorable for reducing the incidence of chronic disease and mortality. Hence, developing a visual and convenient method for AA assays is of huge significance. Serving a reducing substance, AA is selected as a model target and added into the developed Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system for visible imaging assays. The luminescence brightness decreases with increase of AA addition (Fig. 4C). The light emission intensity ratio exhibits linearity with the concentration of AA ranging from 1μM to100 μM (Fig. 4D). The linear regression equation was calculated as I/I0 =−0.006 [AA] +0.952 with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.5μM (S/N=3). I0 and I represents to the signal intensity value form circular selected area of CL images that obtained for the condition Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system in the absence and presence of AA addition. This linear correlation indicates that Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system could be used for visible quantification analysis of AA. The method can be conducted in 96-well plate for high-through analysis which is convenient for larger bath detection. Compared with other nano-material based methods for AA detection, this chemiluminescent imaging method shows comparable or even better response (Table S1).

In order to expand the CL imaging system to detect AA in real-sample, other interferential reducing concomitants containing in fruit was investigated. As showed in Fig. 5A, AA can effectively quench the CL of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system and exhibit good selectivity comparing to other reducing concomitants such as glucose and other acids. AA content in three kinds of samples, such as grapefruit, orange and sweet orange was imaged to verify the feasibility of the proposed ECL system (Fig. 5B). The contents of each sample calculated from the calibration curve were compared with those obtained using the AA detection kit method, and the data in Table 1. The detection results obtained from the proposed method are comparable to that of commercial method with AA kit, validating the feasibility of the proposed ECL system. In order to broaden its application, the proposed system was used for commercially available Vc pills and drinks detection and the relevant results were showed in Fig. S9 and tabulated in Table S2. Both the detected AA content of Vc drinks and Vc pills obtained from the proposed method are comparable to the given levels, validating the widely application. In addition, comparing to general single sample analysis of commercial kit method, the proposed method is convenient for larger bath detection because of the ability for high-through analysis. Furthermore, the RSD for 10 parallel experiments with 96-well plates by 50μM AA additions was 4.5%, indicating high reproducibility and stability because of the long-persistent CL emission of Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system. In summary, the proposed Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 CL system for AA imaging has high stability and good reproducibility which guarantee its application in practical sample.

In summary, NGQDs was fabricated and demonstrated to generate CL under the direct effects of H2O2. In addition, Cu2+ ion can enlarge the CL intensity of NGQDs 214 times for its catalysis Fenton-like reaction for H2O2 decomposition, which displaying unique specificity against other metal ions. Afterward, the mechanisms of Cu2+ ion enhanced CL of NGQDs/H2O2 system are discussed in details. Furthermore, the developed Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 system emitting persistent light studied as an imaging model was applied for visual imaging sensing. Finally, an imaging sensing method based on Cu2+/NGQDs/H2O2 CL system is developed for AA detection and applied in real fruit sample. The CL imaging method demonstrated with high stability and proper sensitivity providing a convenient and visual tool for AA determination would be a promising candidate for imaging sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21305037, 21475037, and 21235002), the Excellent Youth Foundation of Hunan Scientific Committee (No. 2015JJ1005), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.01.061.

ce:section.ce:para:
